title: Closures Practice
instructions: Create closures and function factories using nested functions and nonlocal variables.
hints: Inner functions can access variables from outer functions
Use nonlocal to modify enclosing scope variables
Return the inner function (without calling it) to create a closure

testCases.description: make_multiplier should return a function that multiplies by the given factor
testCases.validation:
double = make_multiplier(2)
assert double(5) == 10, 'Doubler should return 10 for input 5'

testCases.description: make_multiplier should work with different factors
testCases.validation:
triple = make_multiplier(3)
assert triple(4) == 12, 'Tripler should return 12 for input 4'

testCases.description: make_counter should return incrementing values starting from start
testCases.validation:
counter = make_counter()
assert counter() == 0 and counter() == 1 and counter() == 2, 'Counter should return 0, 1, 2 on successive calls'

testCases.description: create_greeter should return a function that greets with custom greeting
testCases.validation:
hello = create_greeter('Hello')
assert hello('World') == 'Hello, World!', 'Should greet with Hello, World!'

testCases.description: make_counter should support custom start and step values
testCases.validation:
counter = make_counter(start=10, step=5)
assert counter() == 10 and counter() == 15, 'Should start at 10 and increment by 5'

testCases.description: create_greeter closures should be independent of each other
testCases.validation:
hi = create_greeter('Hi')
hey = create_greeter('Hey')
assert hi('Alice') == 'Hi, Alice!' and hey('Bob') == 'Hey, Bob!', 'Each greeter should maintain its own greeting'

description: Create closures and function factories using nested functions and the nonlocal keyword. Build a multiplier factory, a stateful counter with configurable start and step, and a customizable greeter — demonstrating how inner functions capture and retain variables from their enclosing scope.
